question: Which type of mirror is used in the following? Street lights
answer: Convex mirror
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Reflection of Light

question: Which type of mirror is used in the following? Head lamps of a car
answer: Concave mirror
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Reflection of Light

question: An object of height 7 cm is kept at a distance of 25 cm in front of a concave mirror. The focal length of the mirror is 15 cm. At what distance from the mirror should
answer:
Given Data:

Object height (ho) = 7 cm
Object distance (u) = −25 cm (since the object is in front of the concave mirror, it is taken as negative)
Focal length (f) = −15 cm (concave mirror has a negative focal length)

Step 1: Find the Image Distance v
1/f = 1/v + 1/u
1/v = 1/f - 1/u
1/v = 1/-15 - 1/-25
1/v = 1/-15 + 1/25
1/v = (-5+3)/75 = (-2)/75
v = −37.5 cm
Step 2: Find the Size of the Image
Using the magnification formula:
m = -v/u
m = -(-37.5)/-25
= −1.5
Now, calculating the image height:
himage​ = m × hobject​
himage​ = −1.5 × 7
= −10.5 cm
Step 3: Nature of the Image

Since v is negative, the image is real and inverted.
Since magnification ∣m∣ = 1.5 is greater than 1, the image is magnified.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Reflection of Light

question: A convex mirror has a focal length of 18 cm. The image of an object kept in front of the mirror is half the height of the object. What is the distance of the object from the mirror?
answer:
Given:
Focal length (f) = 18 cm
Magnifiction (M) = ("Height of the image" ("h"₂))/("Height of the object" ("h"₁))
= 1/2
Find: Object distance (u)
Formula:

M = ("h"₂)/("h"₁) = -"v"/"u"
1/"f" = 1/"v" + 1/"u"

Calculations:
According to formula (i),
1/2 = -"v"/"u"
∴ v = -"u"/2
According to formula (ii),
1/"f" = 1/"v" + 1/"u" = ("u" + "v")/("uv")
∴ f = ("uv")/ ("u" + "v")
Now, v = -"u"/2
∴ f = ("u"((-"u")/2))/("u" + ((-"u")/2))
∴ 18 = ((-"u"²)/2)/((2"u" - "u")/2) = (-"u"²)/"u"
∴ u = −18 cm
A negative sign indicates that the object is placed to the left of the mirror.
Hence, the distance of the object from the mirror is 18 cm.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Reflection of Light

question: A 10 cm long stick is kept in front of a concave mirror having a focal length of 10 cm in such a way that the end of the stick closest to the pole is at a distance of 20 cm.
answer:
Given:
Length of stick = 10 cm
Distance of nearer end from mirror = 20 cm
So, distance of farther end = 20 + 10 = 30 cm
Focal length of concave mirror = 10 cm
Use the mirror formula:
1/f = 1/v + 1/u
Step 1: Image of the nearest end (u1 = 20 cm)
1/10 = 1/v₁ + 1/20
1/v₁ =1/10 - 1/20 = 1/20
v1 ​= 20 cm
Step 2: Image of the farthest end (u2 = 30 cm)
1/10 = 1/v₂ + 1/30
1/v₂ = 1/10 - 1/30 = 2/30 = 1/15
v2​ = 15 cm
Step 3: Length of the image
The two image points form at 15 cm and 20 cm from the mirror.
Image length = ∣20 − 15∣ = 5 cm
The length of the image is 5 cm.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Reflection of Light

question: Three mirrors are created from a single sphere. Which of the following - pole, centre of curvature, radius of curvature, principal axis - will be common to them and which will not be common?
answer:
Let us name the mirrors M1, M2 and M3 as shown below.

From the figure, we see that

pole (P) is not common.
centre of curvature (C) is common.
principal axis (PA) is not common.
radius of curvature (R) is common. The radius of curvature is the radius of the sphere. In the given figure, the length CP is the same for all mirrors.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Reflection of Light

question: Explain by giving a scientific reason. The roof of a movie theatre and a conference hall is curved.
answer: Ceiling of a movie theatre and conference hall is curved to avoid undesirable reverberation. Otherwise, pitch or music will not be heard distinctly.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Study of Sound

question: Explain by giving a scientific reason. The intensity of reverberation is higher in a closed and empty house.
answer: In an empty house, there are no sound absorbers present such as furniture, curtains etc. Also, if the room is closed, the sound cannot escape the room. Thus, the sound goes through multiple reflections from the rigid walls of a closed and empty room without the reflections of the sound being absorbed. Hence, the intensity of reflections does not fade which would have faded if the room was not empty (has sound absorbers). Due to this, the intensity of reverberation is higher in a closed and empty room.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Study of Sound

question: Explain by giving a scientific reason. We cannot hear the echo produced in a classroom.
answer:
n echo is a sound produced when the original sound is reflected by the walls of a given room. For instance, in a small room If there are hard walls there will be an echo of sound bouncing off the wall but because the sound bouncing is so close to the original, it is harder to detect the echo produced.
A room which is having a dimension greater than 17.2 m will produce an echo in that room.
The dimensions of a classroom usually do not exceed 17.2 m, so echo is not produced in a classroom. So, in the case of a classroom, no echo is produced in a classroom.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Study of Sound

question: Answer the following question in your own words. What is an echo?
answer: n echo is the reflection of sound waves off a surface, such as a wall, mountain, or building, that returns to the listener after a delay. It occurs when the reflected sound reaches the ear after at least 0.1 seconds, allowing the listener to distinguish it from the original sound. Echoes are commonly experienced in large, empty spaces or near hard, smooth surfaces.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Study of Sound

question: Answer the following question in your own words. What factors are important to get a distinct echo?
answer:
mbient temperature and the distance between the sound source and the reflecting surface are two factors that are important in the formation of echoes.
The speed of sound in air at 22°C is 344 m/s. Our brain retains a sound for 0.1 seconds, so only if the sound reaches the barrier and reaches the listener's ears after more than 0.1 seconds will we hear it as a separate sound.
Distance = Speed × Time 
= 344 m/sec × 0.1 sec 
= 34.4 m 
Thus, the distance from the source of the sound to the obstacle and back to the source is the distance that the sound must travel at least 34.4 m.
So the minimum distance from the sound source to the obstacle should be half of the above distance i.e. 17.2 meters to hear a clear echo.
These distances are different at different temperatures.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Study of Sound

question: Answer the following question in your own words. Study the construction of the Golghumat at Vijapur and discuss the reasons for the multiple echoes produced there.
answer: In Vijaypur, Karnataka, there is an ancient site called Golghumat. The inside of its big circle is smooth and round, with a cap on top of it. Since the structure is circular, sound waves that start at one point are mirrored many times before going back to the same point, where they are heard more than once.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Study of Sound

question: Answer the following question in your own words. What should be the dimensions and the shape of classrooms so that no echo can be produced there?
answer:
mbient temperature and the distance between the source and the reflecting surface are important factors in the generation of echoes. A distance of at least 17.2 m between the source and the reflecting surface is required for a clear echo to be heard at 22°C.
The classroom must be designed in such a way that the distance between two walls is less than 17.2 meters to avoid the formation of echoes.
Also, the ceiling should be curved so that the reflection of sound waves is uniform throughout the room.
Classrooms are generally rectangular in design to avoid echo generation.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Study of Sound

question: Where and why are sound-absorbing materials used?
answer:
Sound absorbing materials are used in concert halls or auditoriums to reduce high-intensity reverberation.
The ceiling or walls of the auditorium are covered with sound absorbing materials like thermocol, fiber board, rough plaster.
The seats are also made of sound-absorbing material, which reduces additional reverberation.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Study of Sound

question: The speed of sound in air at 0°C is 332 m/s. If it increases at the rate of 0.6 m/s per degree, what will be the temperature when the velocity has increased to 344 m/s?
answer:
Let the temperature at which the velocity of air becomes 344 m/s be T°C.
The increase in velocity of sound in air from 0°C to T°C = 344 - 332 = 12 m/s
Given,
The velocity of sound in air increases by 0.6 m/s for a 1°C rise in temperature.
Therefore, for a 1 m/s increase in the velocity of sound in air, the temperature should rise by (1/0.6) ᶜⁱʳᶜC.
For a 12 m/s increase in the velocity of sound in air, the temperature should rise by = (12/0.6) ᶜⁱʳᶜ"C"
= (12 xx 10)/(0.6 xx 10)
= 120/6
= 20ᶜⁱʳᶜ "C"
Thus the speed of sound at 20°C will be 344 m/s.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Study of Sound

question: Nita heard the sound of lightning after 4 seconds of seeing it. What was the distance of the lightning from her? (The velocity of sound in air is 340 m/s.)
answer:
Given:

Speed of sound in air (v) = 340 m/s,
Time (T) = 4 second

Find: Distance (λ)
Formula: v = lambda/"T"
Calculations:
According to formula,
340 = lambda/4
λ = 340 × 4
λ = 1360 m
The distance of lightning flash from Nita will be 1360 m.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Study of Sound

question: Sunil is standing between two walls. The wall closest to him is at a distance of 360 m. If he shouts, he hears the first echo after 4 s and another after another 2 seconds.
answer:
Given - a distance of the nearest wall from Sunil = 660 m
Time after which Sunil hears first echo, (t1) = (4 sec)/(2 sec) = 2 s
Time after which Sunil hears second echo (t2) = (6 sec)/(2 sec) = 3 s
Velocity = "distance"/"time"
Let d1 be the distance of closest wall.
Let d2 be the distance of the other closest wall.
V = "d"₁/"t"₁ = 660/2 = 330 m/s
Velocity = 330 m/s
Velocity = "d"₂/"t"₂
330 = "d"₂/3
330 × 3 = "d"₂
"d"₂ = 990
Distance between the two walls -
d1 + d2 = 660 + 990 = 1650 m
Distance between the two walls - 1650 m
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Study of Sound

question: Hydrogen gas is filled in two identical bottles, A and B, at the same temperature. The mass of hydrogen in the two bottles is 12 gm and 48 gm respectively. In which bottle will sound travel faster?
answer:
Mass of hydrogen in bottle A = w1 = 12gm
Mass of hydrogen in bottle B = w2 = 48gm
∴ Density ∝ mass
"P"₁/"P"₂="w"₁/"w"₂ and for velocity "v" ∝ 1/√(ρ)∝1/√"w" 
∴ "v"₁/"v"₂ = √("P"₂/"P"₁) = √("w"₂/"w"₁)= √((48)/(12)) = √(4/1) = 2/1
Hence, Sound will travel faster in bottle A and the speed will be twice as that of bottle B.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Study of Sound

question: Helium gas is filled in two identical bottles A and B. The mass of the gas in the two bottles is 10 gm and 40 gm respectively. If the speed of sound is the same in both bottles, what conclusions
answer:
Let the gas filled bottles be A and B respectively.
Mass of the gas in bottle A = 10 g
Mass of gas in bottle B = 40 g
The velocity of sound in gas is related to the density of gas as
v ∝ 1/√(ρ) ...... (i)
and the velocity of sound in a gas is related to the temperature of the gas as 
v ∝ √(T) ...... (ii)
Combining (i) and (ii), we get
v ∝ √(T)/√(ρ) 
The bottles are identical, which means the volumes of the gases are equal. Let the volume of the bottle be V. Let M1 and M2 be the masses of gases in bottles A and B, respectively and v1 and v2 be the velocity of the sound in the two bottles, respectively. Also, let T1 and T2 be their respective temperatures. Therefore,
v₁/v₂ = (√(M₂/V) √(T₁))/(√(M₁/V) √(T₂))
Now, given that, "v"₁ = "v"₂
⇒ √(M₁)√(T₂) = √(M₂)√(T₁)
or , T₂ = (M₂T₁)/M₁
Given , M₁ = 10 "g" , M₂ = 40 "g"
= T₂ = (40T₁)/10 = 4T₁
Thus, it can be concluded that the temperature of bottle B is 4 times the temperature of A.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Study of Sound

question: Why are carbon and its compounds used as fuels?
answer:
The following explanations explain why fuels made of carbon and its compounds are used:

Because of their high hydrogen and carbon content, they provide combustion with a significant amount of heat.
Fuel-grade carbon compounds are straightforward to work with and have high calorific values and ideal ignition temperatures.
There is control over their combustion. Carbon and its components are therefore employed as fuels.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: In which compound forms does carbon occur?
answer:
Carbon occurs in the following compounds:

Hydrocarbons: These are organic compounds which are made up of carbon and hydrogen.
CO2: Carbon also exists as carbon dioxide and occurs in the air in a free state. it is also found as a salt in chalk and limestone.
Fossil fuel: coal, petroleum, natural gas.
Carbonaceous nutrients: carbohydrates, proteins, fats.
Natural fibres: cotton, wool, silk.
CH4: Carbon also exists as methane.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: Write the uses of diamond.
answer:
The uses of diamonds are as follows:

Diamonds are used in jewellery because of their bright shine and attractive appearance.
Due to their hardness, diamonds are used in cutting glass, drilling rocks, and shaping metals.
Diamond powder (diamond dust) is used for polishing other diamonds and hard surfaces.
Diamonds are used in making special windows in satellites to protect instruments from radiation.
Very fine diamond blades are used in delicate eye-surgery instruments.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: Explain the difference: Diamond and graphite
answer: Diamond
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: Explain the difference. Crystalline and non-crystalline forms of carbon.
answer:
Crystalline carbon
Non-crystalline carbon

A crystalline form has a regular and definite arrangement of atoms.
A Non-crystalline form does not have the regular arrangement of atoms.

Their melting point and boiling point are high and specific.
It melts at very high temperatures.

Crystalline carbon is a very hard substance.
Non-crystalline carbon is brittle.

A crystalline form has a definite geometrical shape, sharp edges and plane surfaces.
They are amorphous, hence, they do not have definite geometrical shape.

Diamond, graphite and fullerene are different crystalline forms of carbon.
Coal, charcoal and coke are different non-crystalline/amorphous forms of carbon.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: Write scientific reasons. Graphite is a conductor of electricity.
answer: Graphite is a good conductor of electricity because there are free electrons present between its layers. These free electrons move continuously within the entire layer and thus lead to the conduction of electricity. Hence, graphite is a conductor of electricity.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: Write scientific reasons. Graphite is not used in ornaments.
answer:
The desired characteristic of the material from which jewellery is made is the transparency of the material and the reflection of light from it, But these qualities are not present in graphite.
Moreover, graphite is soft, greyish black, brittle and opaque and not transparent, hard and reflective like a diamond. Hence, Graphite is not used in ornaments.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: Write scientific reasons. Limewater turns milky when CO2 is passed through it.
answer:
When carbon dioxide and CO2 gas are passed through lime water, it turns milky due to the formation of calcium carbonate.
\[\ce{Ca(OH)2_{(aq)} + CO2_{(g)} -> CaCO3_{(s)} + H2O_{(l)}}\]
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: Write scientific reasons. Biogas is an eco-friendly fuel.
answer: Biogas is an eco-friendly fuel because it helps to reduce greenhouse gas emissions and our dependency on fossil fuels. It is produced from the decomposition of organic matter, thus is an effective way of disposal of organic waste.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: Explain the following. Diamond, graphite and fullerenes are crystalline forms of carbon.
answer:
crystalline form of carbon has a regular and definite arrangement of atoms.
In a diamond, each carbon atom is linked to four other carbon atoms by covalent bonds. Therefore, a diamond has a tetragonal three-dimensional structure. Hence a regular and definite structure of atoms is found in diamond.
In graphite, each carbon atom is bonded to three other carbon atoms in such a way that a hexagonal layered structure is formed. Thus, the regular arrangement of atoms in graphite is in layers.
In fullerene, C60 atoms are joined to each other in pentagonal and hexagonal arrangements. As a result, the structure of fullerene is like a football. The molecules of fullerene are found in the form of buckyballs and buckytubes.
Since diamond, graphite and fullerene have definite shapes, sharp edges and plane surfaces, they are in crystalline form. Hence, Diamond, graphite and fullerenes are crystalline forms of carbon.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: Explain the following. Methane is called marsh gas.
answer: Methane is called marsh gas because it is found at the surface of marshy places. A type of wetland is an area of land where water covers the ground for long periods of time, which are usually treeless and is dominated by grasses and herbaceous plants.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: Explain the following. Petrol, diesel, and coal are fossil fuels.
answer:
Fossil fuels are formed from plants, animals and other organisms that have been buried under the ground millions of years ago.
Residues buried in the Earth's interior are weathered by temperature and crustal pressure to form coal, mineral oil (petrol, diesel) and natural gas.
As these substances release energy after combustion, they are used as fuel.
Therefore, petrol, diesel, coal are fossil fuels.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: Explain the following. Uses of various allotropes of carbon.
answer:
Diamond:

used in glass cutting and rock drilling machines.
used in ornaments.
diamond knives are used in eye surgery.
diamond dust is used for polishing other diamonds.
used to make windows give protection from radiation in space and in artificial satellites.

Graphite:

used for making lubricants.
used for making carbon electrodes.
used in pencils for writing.
used in paints and polish.
used in arc lamps which give a very bright light.

Fullerene:

Fullerenes are used as insulators.
Fullerenes are used as a catalyst in water purification.
At a certain temperature fullerene exhibits superconductivity.

Coal:

Coal is used as fuel in factories and homes.
Coal is used to obtain coke, coal gas and coal tar.
Coal is used in thermal power plants for generation of electricity.
Charcoal is used in the purification of water and organic material.

Coke:

Used as domestic fuel.
Coke is used as a reducing agent.
Coke is used in the production of aeriform fuel such as water gas (CO + H2) and producer gas (CO + H2 + CO2 + N2).
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: Explain the following. Use of CO2 in fire extinguisher.
answer:
Fuel, oxygen, and heat are the three components that cause fire. Fire will cease if any one of the three components is eliminated. One element, oxygen, is eliminated by carbon dioxide.
Because CO2 is heavier than oxygen, it blankets the fire. The fire is contained since there is no longer any contact between the fuel and oxygen. Consequently, fire extinguishers use CO2.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: Explain the following. Practical uses of CO2.
answer:
The practical uses of CO2 are:

It is used for getting special effects of a mist in dramas and movies.
It is used to make aerated drinks.
Solid carbon dioxide is used in cold storage and also to keep milk and milk products and frozen substances cool during transport.
Liquid CO2 is used as a solvent in modern eco-friendly dry cleaning.
CO2 obtained by chemical reaction or kept under pressure is used in fire extinguishers.
Plants use CO2 in the air for photosynthesis.
Liquid CO2 is used to remove caffeine from coffee.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: Write two physical properties. Diamond
answer:
Physical properties of diamond:

Brilliant and pure diamond is the hardest natural substance.
Diamond is a bad conductor of electricity as it does not have free electrons.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: Write two physical properties. Charcoal
answer:
Physical properties of charcoal:

Light in weight, and black in colour, charcoal is an amorphous form of carbon.
It is a bad conductor of heat and electricity.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: Write two physical properties which are given below. Fullerene
answer:
Physical properties of fullerene:

They exist in the form of buckyballs and buckytubes.
They are made up of 60 C atoms.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: What are the different types of coal?
answer:
Coal is a fossil fuel. It contains carbon, hydrogen and oxygen. It also contains nitrogen, phosphorus and sulphur. It occurs in the solid state.
Coal is classified on the basis of its carbon content into:

Peat: It has a high water content and less than 60% carbon content.
Lignite: Lignite is a transformed form of peat and has around 60 to 70% of carbon content.
Bituminous: It is the most commonly found variety of coal in India is bituminous and has a carbon content of 70-90%.
Anthracite: It has a carbon content of more than 95% which makes it suitable for iron smelting.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: What are the uses of coal?
answer:
Coal is used as fuel in factories and homes.
Coal is used to obtain coke, coal gas and coal tar.
Coal is used in thermal power plants for the generation of electricity.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: How will you prove experimentally that graphite is a good conductor of electricity?
answer:
Objective: To prove that graphite is a conductor of electricity.
Materials: Pencil, conductive wire, battery/cell, small bulb.
Action: Sharpen the pencil on both sides. Now, connect the two ends of the pencil with electrical wires. Connect one end of the wire to the cell/battery and the other end to the small bulb. Connect the other side of the bulb and the other side of the battery with another short wire. Start the current in the circuit and observe.
Diagram:

An electric current flows through the graphite. 
Observation: An electric bulb in the circuit lights up.
Inference/Conclusion: This experiment proves that graphite in pencil is a good conductor of electricity.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: Explain the properties of carbon.
answer:
Carbon exhibits allotropy. It exists in more than one form The chemical properties of these different forms are the same but their physical properties are different.
Carbon allotropes have two forms: crystalline form and amorphous form.

Crystalline form:

A crystalline form has a regular and definite arrangement of atoms. 
They have high melting points and boiling points.
A crystalline form has a definite geometrical shape, sharp edges and plane surfaces.
Carbon has three crystalline allotropes.

Diamond
Graphite
Fullerene

Non-crystalline form: The arrangement of carbon atoms is irregular. Examples: Coal, Charcoal, Coke.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: Classify carbon.
answer:
Carbon exists in two different forms i.e. allotropic and amorphous forms.

Crystalline forms:

Diamond: In diamond, each atom of carbon of the given crystal unit is surrounded by four other carbon atoms, which are joined by covalent bonds, such that they form a regular tetrahedron. The diamond crystal is a compact structure in which atoms of a single unit lie in different planes. Thus, the atoms cannot slip because of their different positions in different planes and hence, diamond is the hardest naturally occurring substance.
Graphite: In graphite, atoms of a single crystal are arranged in a hexagonal ring in a single plane. The bonds between the carbon atoms of two single crystals in the parallel planes are weak. Thus, one plane can easily slide over another plane by applying pressure. This is why graphite is soft and can be used as a lubricant.
Fullerene: It is the third and the most recently discovered allotrope of carbon. Buckminsterfullerene is a cluster of sixty carbon atoms arranged in the form of a football. It is named after the American architect Buckminster Fuller, as it resembled the geodesic dome designed by him. Since it contains sixty carbon atoms, its chemical formula is C60.

Amorphous/Non-crystalline forms:

Coal: It is formed by decomposition of dead remains of plants when they got buried in the land owing to the high temperature and pressure conditions present there. Based on the content of carbon present, normally four types of coal deposits exist and these are as follows:

Peat with less than 60% carbon content.
Lignite with 60 to 70% of carbon content.
Bituminous coal with 70 to 90% carbon.
Anthracite is known as the pure form of coal. This coal is hard and contains about 95% of carbon.

Charcoal: It is produced from the bones of animals, combustion of wood etc.
Coke: The pure coal that remains when coal gas has been taken away from coal, is called coke. Coke is used in production of aeriform fuel such as water gas (CO + H2) and producer gas (CO + H2 + CO2 + N2).
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: How will you verify the properties of carbon dioxide?
answer:
Methods to verify the properties of carbon dioxide are:

By using limewater:
Step 1: Take some limewater in a test tube.
Step 2: Take some baking soda in another test tube and add lemon juice to it. Fit the bent tube over it.
Step 3: Insert its other end into the lime water.
Observations: We will observe that limewater turns milky as it comes in contact with the released gas in the form of effervescence. When limewater turns milky, it is confirmed that the effervescence released is of carbon dioxide gas.
The reaction taking place:
\[\ce{Ca(OH)2 + CO2 -> CaCO3 + H2O }\]
Sodium carbonate is formed when carbon dioxide is passed through an aqueous solution of sodium hydroxide.
The reaction taking place:
\[\ce{2NaOH + CO2 -> Na2CO3 + H2O}\]
Sodium bicarbonate is formed by passing CO2 through an aqueous solution of sodium carbonate.
The reaction taking place:
\[\ce{Na2CO3 + H2O + CO2 -> 2NaHCO3}\]
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Carbon: An important element

question: What is meant by radioactivity?
answer: Elements with a high atomic number such as uranium, thorium, radium have a property of spontaneously emitting invisible, highly penetrating and high energy radiation. This property is called radioactivity. The nucleus of radioactive elements is unstable.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Substances in Common Use

question: When is said to be the nucleus unstable?
answer: The nucleus is said to be unstable when radiations (alpha, beta, gamma rays) are emitted by the nucleus.

question: Which diseases are caused by artificial food colours?
answer: Tetrazene, sunset yellow is an artificial food colours that is used extensively. Over-consumption of artificial food colours can be detrimental to health. Food colours that are added to pickles, jam, and sauce may contain small quantities of lead and mercury, which can be harmful to those who consume these products on a regular basis. The Consumption of food with artificial food colours can result in the development of diseases like ADHD (Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder) which can affect children.

question: Write down properties of teflon.
answer:
1Show Solution
It is inert to the atmosphere and chemical substances.
Both water and oil do not stick to the Teflon-coated articles.
It has a high melting point, i.e. 327°C, which means there is no effect of high temperatures on it.
It is easier to clean Teflon-coated articles.

question: What type of colours will you use to celebrate ecofriendly Rang Panchami? Why?
answer: We should use natural colours for celebrating an eco-friendly Rang Panchami. These natural colours can be made from natural ingredients like beetroot, flowers of the flame of the forest, spinach, flame tree (Gulmohar), etc. These are natural colours that do not pose any threat to our health or the environment. The synthetic/artificial colours which we use are a serious threat to our health as they can lead to problems like blindness, skin cancer, asthma, itching of the skin, permanent blocking of sweat pores, etc.

question: Why has the use of methods like Teflon coating become more common?
answer: Teflon is a substance that is inert to chemicals, stable at higher temperatures, easy to clean, and non-stick. All these properties make it an ideal substance for coating. The use of Teflon coating for cooking utensils and industrial equipment has become more common to avoid sticking.

question: Give a scientific explanation. Bleaching powder has the odour of chlorine.
answer:
Chlorine is the main ingredient in bleaching powder. When exposed to air, bleaching powder reacts with carbon dioxide in the air to produce calcium carbonate and chlorine. Thus, it smells of chlorine.
CaOCl2 + CO2 -> CaCO3 + Cl2
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Substances in Common Use

question: Give a scientific explanation. The hard water of a well becomes soft on adding washing soda to it.
answer:
The hardness of hard water is due to the presence of chlorides and sulphates of calcium and magnesium. When washing soda (Na2CO3. 10H2O) is added to the hard water, Na2CO3 reacts with the chlorides and sulphates of calcium and magnesium and converts them to insoluble carbonate salts. This changes the hard water into soft water and thus makes it suitable for use.
\[\ce{MgCl2_{(aq)} + Na2CO3_{(s)} → MgCO3_{(s)} + 2NaCl_{(s)} }\]
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Substances in Common Use

question: Give a scientific explanation. Soap forms a precipitate in hard water.
answer: When soap is mixed with hard water calcium and magnesium salts of fatty acids are formed. These being water insoluble they form a precipitate and that is why lather is not formed.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Substances in Common Use

question: Give a scientific explanation. The particles of powder are given an electric charge while spraying them to form the powder coating.
answer: Powder coating is a method of applying a layer harder than paint on the surface of an iron object to prevent rusting. In this method, a polymer resin, a pigment and some other ingredients are melted mixed, cooled and ground into a uniform powder. This powder is sprayed on the polished metal surface by electrostatic spray deposition (ESD). In this method, the particles of the powder are given an electrostatic charge due to which a uniform layer of the powder sticks to the metal surface. Then the object is heated in the oven along with the coating. A chemical reaction occurs in the layer, resulting in the formation of long cross-linked polymeric chains.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Substances in Common Use

question: Give a scientific explanation. The aluminium article is used as an anode in the anodizing process.
answer: In the anodizing process, the aluminium object is immersed in a dilute acid with electrolytic reduction as a positive electrode. When the current is started, hydrogen gas is released near the negative terminal and oxygen gas is released near the positive terminal. A layer of hydrated aluminium oxide is deposited on the aluminium substrate by reacting with oxygen. In this way, anodizing pans, cookers and cooking utensils can be produced.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Substances in Common Use

question: Give a scientific explanation. When the radiation coming out from a certain radioactive substance is passed through an electric field, marks are found at three places on the photographic plate
answer: When the radiation coming out from a certain radioactive substance is passed through an electric field, marks are found at three places on the photographic plate placed in its path. These three marks represent the three kinds of rays which are emitted by the radioactive substances. One type of radiation deviated slightly towards the negatively charged plate and was called alpha rays. The second type of radiation deviated substantially towards the positively charged plate and was called the beta rays. The third type of radiation did not deviate at all and was called the gamma rays.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Substances in Common Use

question: Give a scientific explanation. A certain type of ceramic tile are fixed on the outer layer of a space shuttle.
answer: The specific ceramic tiles used on the outer surface of the Space Shuttle are some Oxides like Alumina (Al2O3), zirconia (ZrO2) Silica (SiO2) and some other compounds like silicon carbide (SiC), boron carbide (B4C) are used instead of clay for making advanced ceramic. This ceramic requires a temperature of 1600 to 1800 °C and an oxygen free atmosphere for firing. The tiles made from these are resistant to all types of gases, water, and acids and are durable in the long run. These tiles are very light and are used as superconductors during disasters.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Substances in Common Use

question: Write about artificial food colours, the substances used in them and their harmful effects.
answer:
Food colours are often mixed with most of the available foodstuff in the market, such as sweets, soft drinks, cakes, ice creams, meat, spices etc. These food colours are present in the form of powders, gels and pastes. They are used in domestic as well as commercial products. These food colours can be natural as well as artificial in nature.
Tartrazine and sunset yellow are extensively used artificial food colours.
While natural food colours are harmless and can be consumed without worry, artificial food colours, on the other hand, do have some adverse effects. Consuming these regularly is harmful to us as they contain small quantities of lead and mercury.
Excessive consumption of foods with added artificial colours can cause diseases like ADHD (Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder) in children.

question: Give examples of salts with water of crystallization, and their uses.
answer:
lum (Potash alum - K2SO4.Al2(SO4)3.24H2O)
Borax (Na2B4O7.10H2O)
Epsom salt (Magnesium sulphate - MgSO4.7H2O)
Barium chloride (BaCl2.2H2O)
Sodium sulphate (Glauber’s salt Na2SO4.10H2O)
Blue vitriol (CuSO4.5H2O)

Uses:

Alum: Alum and burnt alum is used in medicines. In the paper industry, alum is used to give shine to the paper. Alum is used to stop bleeding from wound. Alum is used in the process of purification of water.

Borax: Borax is used in the cosmetic industry to make lotions, shampoos and cold creams. It is used to remove the stain on clothes in a laundry. In the laboratory with the help of borax bead test, basic elements from the coloured salts are determined.

Epsom salt: Epsom salt is used in the manufacture of fertilizers and also to enhance the green colour of plants and vegetables. It is used in the manufacture of purgative medicines. It is used as body scrub. It is added in bath water (salt bath) to relieve muscle pain.
Barium chloride: It is used in the purification of brine solution. (saturated sodium chloride solution). It is used in the waste water management. It is used in the paper industry and also used in the dye industry, ceramics, oil purification and pesticides.
Sodium sulphate: It is used in the preparation of detergents. It is used to make paper pulp in paper industry and also used in the dye in the dye industry. It is used to make mild purgatives.

Blue vitriol: It is used in the blood test to diagnose anaemia. Blue vitriol and slaked lime are used in the Bordeaux mixture, which is used as a fungicide on fruits.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Substances in Common Use

question: Write briefly about the three methods of electrolysis of sodium chloride.

fter preparing a solution by mixing very low sodium chloride (salt) in water, when electric current is passed, this solution gets decomposed and hydrogen gas is produced near the negative terminal and oxygen gas near the positive terminal.
Chlorine gas is liberated when electricity is passed through a saturated aqueous solution of sodium chloride (brine – 10% NaCl).
On electrolysis of molten sodium chloride, chlorine gas is released near the positive side and liquid sodium metal is released near the negative side.

question: Write the uses of Radioactive substances.
answer:
Medical Applications:

Cancer Treatment: Radioactive isotopes such as cobalt-60 and iodine-131 are used to treat cancer through radiation.
Diagnostic Imaging: Medical imaging uses radioisotopes like Technetium-99m to identify conditions by tracing organ function. 

Industrial applications: 

Material Testing: Non-destructive testing (NDT) uses radioactive sources to test materials and buildings for defects.
Thickness Gauges: Radioisotopes are used in manufacturing to measure the thickness of materials such as paper, plastic, and metal.

Energy Production:

Nuclear Power: Uranium-235 and Plutonium-239 are used as fuel in nuclear reactors to produce power.

Agriculture:

Food Irradiation: Radioactive materials keep food safe by destroying bacteria, bugs, and other pathogens.
Tracer Studies: Radioisotopes investigate nitrogen uptake in plants and optimize fertilizer use.

Scientific Research:

Radiocarbon dating: It uses carbon-14 to calculate the age of fossils and archaeological objects.
Tracer Techniques: Methods used in research to track the transport of components in biochemical and environmental processes.

Space exploration:

Power Sources: Radioisotope thermoelectric generators (RTGs) supply energy for spacecraft and probes when solar power is unavailable.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Substances in Common Use

question: Write the harmful effects. Radioactive substances
answer:
The central nervous system is affected by radioactive radiations.
Hereditary defects are generated by bombardment of radiation on D.N.A in the body.
Radioactive radiation can penetrate the skin, and causes diseases like skin cancer, leukemia.
The radiative pollutants created due to explosions enter the body through air and it is difficult to control them.
The radioactive pollutants released in the sea enter the bodies of fishes and through them enter the human body.
The radioactive paint on the watch can cause cancer.
The radioactive isotopes strontium-90 can enter the body through plants, fruits, flowers, cereals, milk, etc. and cause diseases like bone cancer, leukemia.

question: Match the Pairs and Explain. ‘A’  - 1. Growth of pollen tube towards ovule, 2. Growth of shoot system, 3. Growth of root system, 4. Growth towards water
answer:
‘A’ 
Answers
Explanation

1. Growth of pollen tube towards ovule 
b. Chemotropic movement

Chemotropism refers to the growth of plants navigated by chemical stimulus from outside of the plant.

2. Growth of shoot system 
c. Phototropic movement

Phototropism refers to the growth of a plant in response to a light stimulus.

3. Growth of root system 
a. Gravitropic movement

Gravitropism refers to the growth of a plant in response to gravity pulling on it.

 4. Growth towards water 
e. Hydrotropic movement

Hydrotropism refers to the growth of plants in response to the direction determined by a stimulus or gradient in water concentration.

question: Complete the paragraph. The milk was on the stove. Rasika was engrossed in watching television. She smelled something burning. She ran towards the kitchen. The milk was boiling over.
answer: The milk was on the stove. Rasika was engrossed in watching television. She smelled something burning. She ran towards the kitchen. The milk was boiling over. She held the vessel with her bare hands but, screaming, she let it go at once. This activity was controlled by nerve cells. Special ends of dendrites in these cells collected the information, from where it was transferred to the cell body and then towards the terminal end of the axon. The chemicals produced at the terminal end passed through the minute space i.e. synapse. In this way, impulses were conducted in the body and the process of reflex action was completed by conducting the impulses from nerve to muscle cells.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Life Processes in Living Organisms

question: Write a note on: Root pressure
answer: Root cells are in contact with water and minerals in the soil. Water and minerals enter the cells on the root surface due to differences in concentration. As a result, these cells become turgid. These turgid cells exert pressure on the adjacent cells. This is called ‘root pressure’. Under the effect of this pressure, water and minerals reach the xylem of the roots and to reduce this difference in concentration they are continuously pushed forward. As a result of this continuous movement, a water column is formed, which is continuously pushed ahead. This pressure is sufficient to lift the water up in shrubs, small plants and small trees.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Life Processes in Living Organisms

question: Write a note on: Transpiration
answer: Plants give out water in the form of vapour through the stomata on their leaves. Two cells called guard cells are present around the stomata. These cells control the opening and closing of the stomata. Transpiration occurs through these stomata. Water is released into the atmosphere by leaves through the process of evaporation. As a result, the water level in the epidermal layer of the leaf decreases. Water is brought up to the leaves through the xylem so as to compensate for the lost water. Transpiration helps in the absorption of water and minerals and distribution to all parts of the plant whereas root pressure performs the important role of pushing the water up during the night time.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Life Processes in Living Organisms

question: Write a note on: Nerve cell
answer: Special types of cells which conduct impulses from one place to another in the body are called neurons. Neurons are the structural and functional units of the nervous system. Nerve cells, the largest cells in the human body, may measure up to a few metres in length. Nerve cells have the ability to generate and conduct electrochemical impulses. All the information about our surroundings is collected by the ends or dendrites of the neuron. The chemical process begins at those ends and electric impulses are generated which are conducted from the dendrites to the cell body, from the cell body to the axon and from the axon to its terminal. These impulses are then to be transferred from this nerve cell to the next. The cells that support the nerve cells and help in their functioning are called neuroglia. Nerve cells and neuroglial cells together form the nerves.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Life Processes in Living Organisms

question: Write a note on. Human brain
answer:
Human brain:
The human brain is the control center of the body, responsible for thinking, emotions, memory, and body functions. It is protected by the skull and is a part of the central nervous system (CNS) along with the spinal cord.
Main Parts of the Human Brain:

Cerebrum: The largest part, responsible for thinking, memory, emotions, and voluntary actions.
Cerebellum: Controls balance, coordination, and muscle movements.
Brainstem: Regulates breathing, heartbeat, and other involuntary functions.

Functions of the Brain:

Controls movement, speech, and decision-making.
Processes sensory information (vision, hearing, touch, smell, and taste).
Regulates body temperature, hunger, and sleep.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Life Processes in Living Organisms

question: Write a note on. Reflex action
answer:
n immediate and involuntary response given to a stimulus from the environment is called a reflex action.
Sometimes we react to an incident without any thinking on our part or control over the reaction. This is a response given to a certain stimulus from the surroundings.
In such situations, proper control and coordination are achieved even without the brain’s intervention.
The path of reflex action is as follows: This is also known as the reflex arc. Sense organs Sensory neurons → Towards the spinal cord → Coordination neuron → Motor neuron Muscle or gland.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Life Processes in Living Organisms

question: Explain chemical coordination in humans and give the names and functions of some hormones.
answer:
In the human body, chemical control occurs through stimulation, where stimulants are produced from endocrine glands, and chemicals are produced near the contact sites of nerve cells.
Endocrine glands are separate from the duct, and their secretions mix directly with the blood. The receptors of these glands reach all the parts of the body through blood, which are located at certain places in the body.
Endocrine glands work to control and coordinate with the nervous system, and they participate in controlling and coordinating various functions of the body, for which there is cooperation between these two organizations.
When various activities begin, stimulants are secreted as per requirement. The amount and timing of secretion of the propellant is always regularly controlled.
Pituitary, pituitary gland, adrenal glands, taste buds, some cells, gonads - ovaries, testicles, and thymus glands are all important endocrine glands.
Growth stimulant, adrenal gland stimulant, thyroid gland stimulant, prolactin, oxytocin, luteinizing hormone, antitumor stimulant, follicular gland stimulant, these are the stimulants of pituitary gland. Of these, growth promoters are essential for the growth of the body. Prolactin, oxytocin, luteinizing hormone, follicle stimulating hormone, these hormones are especially important for the reproduction of women's body.
Estrogen and progesterone, which are produced by the ovaries, are essential for the physical development of women, while testosterone, which is produced by the testicles, is important for men.
The cells of the taste buds produce glucagon and insulin and regulate blood sugar levels.
The thyroid gland significantly produces thyroxine and calcitonin. Due to thyroxine, the metabolic process in its body remains functioning smoothly.
Adrenaline, noradrenaline, and corticosteroids from the adrenal glands constitutively control many body functions. They include controlling behavior in emergency situations and emotional episodes, stimulating transmission of the cardiovascular and circulatory systems, and stimulating metabolic processes. They also keep the balance of Na and K and metabolism under control.
This type of chemical control is very slow in maintaining body position and coordination, but the process is necessary over a long period of time.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Life Processes in Living Organisms

question: Explain the difference between the excretory system of humans and plants.
answer:
Excretory system in humans
Excretory system in plants

Humans have a well-developed excretory system for the elimination of nitrogenous wastes.
Plants do not have a well-developed excretory system for the elimination of nitrogenous wastes.

The human excretory system comprises the kidneys, blood vessels that join them, ureters, urinary bladder, and urethra.
Plants use different strategies for excretion.

Excretion is in the form of urine produced to filter out the waste products, like urea and uric acid, from the blood.
Excretion is by means of tannins, gums, essential oils, etc stored in special cells.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Life Processes in Living Organisms

question: Explain coordination in plants with the help of suitable examples.
answer:
Since plants do not have nervous and muscular systems like animals, coordination in plants occurs in response to particular stimuli. Tropism/tropic movement is the term used for the movement or growth of any plant part in response to an external stimulus.
These tropic movements can be of various types like:

Phototropic movement: The shoot system of any plant responds to the light stimulus i.e. it grows towards the source of light. The movement shown by plants towards the source of light is called ‘Phototropic movement’.
Gravitropic movement: The root system of plants responds to stimuli like gravitation and water. These responses are called ‘gravitropic movement’
Hydrotropic movement: The root system of plants responds to stimuli like water. These responses are called ‘hydrotropic movement’.
Chemotropic movement: Movement shown by plants in response to specific chemicals is called ‘chemotropic movement’. For exmaple, the growth of the pollen tube towards the ovule.

Each of the above mentioned activities is associated with the growth of plants, hence it is called activities related to their growth. The growth and development process of plants is not related to development. Various types of action are created in the surrounding environment by stimulating vegetation. They use electrical and chemical stimuli, such as mimosa. The amount of water in plant cells is increased or decreased, due to which their shape changes and the formation of activity takes place.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Life Processes in Living Organisms

question: Explain in your own words with a suitable example. What is meant by coordination?
answer:
Coordination is the process of bringing together the actions of several persons or departments to ensure that they function smoothly and efficiently toward a single goal. It helps to reduce confusion, duplication of effort, and delays. When all segments of an organisation work in the same direction, overall performance improves.
Example: During a school’s annual day, instructors, students, stage crew, and management collaborate to organize timing, rehearsals, decorations, and announcements. This organized teamwork demonstrates great coordination.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Life Processes in Living Organisms

question: Explain in your own words with suitable examples. How does excretion occur in human beings?
answer:
The excretory system removes wastes from the body, which is an important function.
The human excretory system is made up of two kidneys, two ureters, the urine bladder, and the urethra. The kidneys produce urine by removing waste and undesirable chemicals from the bloodstream.
Nephrons are the functional units of the kidney that perform the basic function of filtration. The urea generated in the liver enters the bloodstream.
When urea-containing blood enters the glomerulus, it is filtered by capillaries, removing urea and other related compounds.
Water molecules and tiny molecules of other substances can pass through the semipermeable membrane of Bowman's capsule.
The fluid that has accumulated in the Bowman's capsule cavity enters the nephron's tubular portion. Here, water molecules and other important compounds are reabsorbed into the bloodstream.
Urine is generated from the residual fluid, which contains waste elements. The ureters carry urine, which is then stored in the urinary bladder. Urine is then released through the urethra.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Life Processes in Living Organisms

question: Explain in your own words with suitable examples. How is excretion in plants useful to human beings?
answer:
Excretion in plants results in the production of various kinds of excreta which are waste for the plant but are equally important for human use.
The following examples are helpful in proving the above fact:

Oxygen which is an excretory product of photosynthesis is an absolute requirement for human beings to survive on Earth.
Tannin is a waste plant product which is stored in leaves and bark. it is used in making tea all over the world.
Plants store waste products in their leaves and bark which are shed off regularly. These shed-offs are used to make manure.
Gum and resin are waste materials for plants but they have various uses for man.
Essential oils are also waste products of plants which are stored in their leaves. These essential oils are used for various purposes by us.
subject: Science And Tech
chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Life Processes in Living Organisms

question: Explain in your own words with suitable examples. Describe the transportation system in plants.
answer:
Transportation in plants occurs due to the two processes of root pressure and transpiration. Due to this pressure, food is transported as per the requirement of the plant and this process is transferred through sap vessels. In plants, water and food are transported through the vascular system, for which there are water vessels and sap vessels.

Root pressure: Root cells are in contact with water and minerals in the soil. Water and minerals enter the cells on the root surface due to differences in concentration. As a result, these cells become turgid. These turgid cells exert pressure on the adjacent cells. This is called ‘root pressure’. Under the effect of this pressure, water and minerals reach the xylem of the roots and to reduce this difference in concentration they are continuously pushed forward. As a result of this continuous movement, a water column is formed, which is continuously pushed ahead. This pressure is sufficient to lift the water up in shrubs, small plants and small trees.
Transpiration pull: Plants give out water in the form of vapour through the stomata on their leaves. Two cells called guard cells are present around the stomata. These cells control the opening and closing of stomata. Transpiration occurs through these stomata. Water is released into the atmosphere by leaves through the process of evaporation. As a result, water level in the epidermal layer of the leaf decreases. Water is brought up to the leaves through the xylem so as to compensate for the lost water. Transpiration helps in absorption of water and minerals and distribution to all parts of the plant whereas root pressure performs the important role of pushing the water up during the night time.

chapter: Science and Technology [English] 9 Standard Life Processes in Living Organisms
